title: "Three Inspections" Hematoscopy
summary: The repository artifact is the publication-selected University of Michigan CME XML for a May 2020 near-diplomatic comparative transcription of the opening hematoscopy passage of Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting, published with a full header in 2023. Its sixty prose alignment units pair a transcription of British Library Sloane MS 3486, f. 147v, with one of Glasgow University Library MS Hunter 307 (U.7.1), f. 165v line 14 through f. 166r line 25. Hunter 307 continues with scarifying and cupping after the CME301 boundary. A current British Library component record establishes Royal MS 18 A VI, ff. 54r-55v, as a third collateral Middle English witness beginning with a closely corresponding incipit. WebHunter provides public part-based images and a separate transcription of Hunter 307. Its footer offers a broad CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license while its specific Copyright page reserves the images, transcriptions, and site information and requires permission; because the scope conflict is unresolved, no manuscript-image bundle was cached. The complete open 2023 scholarly article and Hunter edition is cached locally, and its three embedded Hunter manuscript plates are modeled separately as a partial facsimile.
review_notes: CME301 is one comparative digital transcription of a coherent work-part represented by two witness streams, not sixty works and not two independently cataloged corpus works.
The Royal MS 18 A VI witness is collateral evidence for the medieval transmission and is not represented as an upstream source of the University of Michigan encoding.

Public access, local caching, copyright, and permission to publish are recorded separately. WebHunter's technically reachable JPEGs were not bulk-cached because its specific Copyright page prohibits copying without permission and the scope of the site's conflicting footer CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license is unresolved.

The sibling wrapper at CME/source/CME_additions/CME301.xml was not modeled as a separate publication ancestor because the repository publication colophon selects CME/source/recent_changes/additions_2023/CME301.xml and the two files share the represented body while differing at the wrapper/header layer.

entities.label: General Corpus publication source XML for CME301
entities.description: The XML artifact selected by the repository publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The pinned file is 9,853 bytes.
Its full 2023 header identifies the two transcribers, the two manuscript locators, the comparative editorial method, and the public-domain availability assertion.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME comparative transcription CME301
entities.description: A near-diplomatic digital transcription pairing the Sloane 3486 and Hunter 307 versions line by line for comparison.
notes: M. Teresa Tavormina transcribed the Sloane stream; Paul F. Schaffner transcribed the Hunter stream and the revision history attributes transcription and markup work to pfs.
Each of the sixty P elements contains one Sloane unit followed by one LB and one Hunter unit. Those P/LB pairs are editorial alignment units, not manuscript line counts.

entities.label: Middle English "Three Inspections" hematoscopy passage
entities.description: The opening passage of the Middle English On Bloodletting tradition explaining how a physician should inspect blood for thickness, temperature, smell, wateriness, surface appearance, color, foam, and other diagnostic signs.
notes: Guy de Chauliac is responsible for the underlying Latin surgical material; the Middle English translator or translators are anonymous.
The evidence does not yet establish whether Sloane, Hunter, and Royal descend from one Middle English translation or from more than one translation or recension.

entities.label: Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting in the Chirurgia magna
entities.description: The bloodletting material attributed to Guy de Chauliac within the Chirurgia magna, the source work behind the Middle English versions.
notes: This abstract source-work entity does not identify the Middle English translator's physical Latin exemplar.

entities.label: British Library, Sloane MS 3486

date_statements.label: date of the target copy reported by the 2023 study
date_statements.value: second or third quarter of the fifteenth century
date_statements.notes: The current BL record dates the composite codex broadly from the thirteenth through seventeenth centuries; this narrower statement is explicitly limited to the target copy.

date_statements.label: MED stencil date preserved in CME
date_statements.notes: This is the corpus's bibliographic stencil, retained separately from the modern specialist dating.

holding.notes: The target passage is on f. 147v; the current parent record describes this composite codex but does not itemize the passage.

notes: The current BL record describes a multilingual medical composite and lists Thomas Betrisden's imperfect work on human diseases at ff. 86-149; the target passage is identified more precisely by the CME XML and the 2023 specialist article.
CME301 transcribes only f. 147v.
The current BL record exposes no Digitised Content for this shelfmark as of 2026-07-14.

entities.label: University of Glasgow Library, MS Hunter 307 (U.7.1)

date_statements.label: MED stencil date preserved in CME

holding.notes: The University of Glasgow's current digitised-collections guide identifies MS Hunter 307 and routes users to the University of Málaga WebHunter project.

physical_description.extent: 167 surviving folios reported by WebHunter; the target treatise occupies ff. 165v-166v
physical_description.dimensions: Binding 200 x 140 mm; leaves 190 x 130 mm; written space 147-150 x 90-95 mm for the target
notes: The target is written in one column of twenty-six lines per folio in black ink, with alternating red and blue paragraph marks and decorated initials.

The 2023 study describes Gothic textura features in the target leaves; WebHunter's broader codex description uses Bastard Anglicana/Anglicana Formata terminology. These source-scoped descriptions are retained rather than forced into a single label.
The manuscript belonged to William Hunter, who bequeathed it to the University of Glasgow; the university has held his legacy since 1807.
The On Bloodletting text begins at f. 165v line 14 and continues through f. 166v.
CME301 stops at f. 166r line 25, immediately before the sentence beginning 'For garsyng and ventosyng' at line 26.
The 2023 dialect study localizes the language of ff. 165v-166v to the Midlands, probably Huntingdonshire; that is a witness-level scribal-language claim, not a work-wide translation origin.

entities.label: British Library, Royal MS 18 A VI

date_statements.label: target component date
date_statements.value: fifteenth century; the two target leaves are in a hand perhaps slightly earlier than the preceding late-fifteenth-century section

physical_description.dimensions: 210 x 140 mm, with smaller later sections
notes: The target component is in Middle English at ff. 54r-55v and follows a treatise on diseases of women.
The current catalog describes the manuscript's origin as England and its script broadly as Gothic cursive.
The exact current catalog component reads: Guy de Chauliac on hematoscopy, scarifying and cupping, translated to Middle English, beginning 'A phisycian behoveth to knowe in manere inspeccions'.

The independently cataloged, closely corresponding incipit and matching content attribution make Royal 18 A VI a secure collateral witness, but CME301 does not transcribe it and the Royal subsection's exact end remains unverified.
No Digitised Content is exposed by the current BL record as of 2026-07-14.

entities.label: WebHunter part-based digital images of MS Hunter 307
entities.reproduction_format: Public 800 x 1140 pixel color JPEG page images arranged in three overlapping text-part sequences
notes: The three WebHunter manifests declare 329 System of Physic pages, 100 Gynaecological Text pages, and 38 Alphabetical List of Drugs pages. Their overlap and transcript-oriented division mean they are not a canonical whole-codex Presentation manifest.

Within hu307p2, provider page 98 (zero-based transcript page 97) is f. 165v and provider page 99 (zero-based transcript page 98) is f. 166r; these are the two leaves containing CME301's Hunter stream.
No local image bundle was created because the site's copyright page reserves all rights in the facsimile images and says images cannot be used without prior written consent.

entities.label: WebHunter diplomatic transcription of MS Hunter 307
notes: WebHunter assigns ff. 149v-166v to a Gynaecological Text transcription by María Laura Esteban Segura, although the 2023 specialist article identifies the final ff. 165v-166v as the distinct Guy de Chauliac On Bloodletting text.
This is a separate digital transcription from the Paul F. Schaffner Hunter stream in CME301.

entities.label: Esteban-Segura and Soriano-Jiménez's 2023 Hunter 307 edition
entities.description: A later semi-diplomatic edition and study of the complete Hunter On Bloodletting text at ff. 165v-166v, including full-page images of the three manuscript sides, codicological and palaeographical analysis, and dialect localization.

bibliographic.citation: Laura Esteban-Segura and Carlos Soriano-Jiménez, 'Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting in Glasgow, University Library, MS Hunter 307 (ff. 165v-166v),' Revista Canaria de Estudios Ingleses 87 (2023): 107-130, https://doi.org/10.25145/j.recaesin.2023.87.06.
bibliographic.title: Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting in Glasgow, University Library, MS Hunter 307 (ff. 165v-166v)

notes: This article postdates the May 2020 CME transcription and is supporting scholarship, not an ancestor of CME301.
Its edition makes the CME boundary auditable: CME301 ends after the Hunter reading 'quesy and viscouse humours' at f. 166r line 25, while the article continues at line 26 and through f. 166v.

entities.label: Hunter 307 ff. 165v-166v plates in the 2023 On Bloodletting article
entities.description: Three full-page manuscript reproductions embedded in Esteban-Segura and Soriano-Jiménez's article, separately modeled from the article's scholarly text and semi-diplomatic edition.
entities.reproduction_format: Full-page color manuscript plates on journal pp. 114, 116, and 118 (PDF pp. 8, 10, and 12)
notes: The three plates reproduce the complete Hunter On Bloodletting leaf run at ff. 165v-166v, but they are partial coverage of the physical manuscript as a whole.
Only f. 165v from line 14 and f. 166r through line 25 belong to CME301; f. 166r line 26 and f. 166v continue with scarifying and cupping outside the corpus selection.
The article states that all manuscript images were reproduced by kind permission of Glasgow University Library's Special Collections.

scope.notes: This relation expresses translation/version identity at the work-part level; it does not identify an extant physical Latin exemplar or require all Middle English witnesses to descend from one translation act.

scope.notes: The plates reproduce all three manuscript sides occupied by the Hunter treatise but only a small part of the complete physical manuscript. CME301 occupies f. 165v line 14 through f. 166r line 25 within those plates.

subject.description: Transmission discussion, edition opening, and boundary evidence

notes: The XML availability statement says U-M provides access to the materials. An automated 2026-07-14 request to the stable handle reached the U-M resolver but was stopped by an anti-bot challenge; the repository copy remains the directly verified text.

access.access_method: WebHunter manuscript viewer and exact static JPEGs
access.format: Part-based viewer with 800 x 1140 JPEG derivatives
notes: The exact target images are hu307p2 pag98-c.jpg (f. 165v) and pag99-c.jpg (f. 166r).
The official Glasgow guide says registration may be required, but the current static manifests, target transcripts, and JPEGs were retrieved without authentication on 2026-07-14.

No local copy is recorded because WebHunter's specific Copyright page prohibits copying and image use without prior written consent, while the footer's broad CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 assertion does not clearly identify whether the manuscript JPEGs are included. Public viewing is not treated as a redistribution license while that conflict remains unresolved.

access.access_method: WebHunter manuscript viewer and exact static transcript pages
notes: Zero-based page97 is f. 165v, page98 is f. 166r, and page99 is f. 166v.

The specific Copyright page prohibits republishing or copying site information without permission, while the site footer broadly applies CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 to The Málaga Corpus; the exact transcription's license scope remains unresolved.

Complete relative to the 24-page article, not to the physical manuscript.

rights.basis: The text is preserved in fifteenth-century manuscripts, and the U-M source header expressly calls the represented materials public domain.
notes: This does not determine rights in modern transcriptions, editions, photographs, catalog descriptions, or provider interfaces.

rights.basis: The Chirurgia magna was composed in 1363 by an author who died in 1368.

rights.component: Modern comparative transcription and markup represented by CME301
rights.basis: The exact U-M header states that the materials are in the public domain and that neither the university nor the editors claims copyright in them.

rights.component: Pinned XML artifact insofar as it reproduces the U-M transcription and markup
rights.basis: The pinned artifact contains the exact U-M public-domain/no-copyright assertion for the represented materials; no stronger claim is made about unrelated repository code or outputs.

rights.jurisdiction: Not jurisdiction-specific in the provider statement
rights.basis: WebHunter states that the facsimile images are © Glasgow University Library and The Wellcome Library, all rights reserved, and that images cannot be used without prior written consent.
notes: The target is a Glasgow manuscript; the provider's combined image-rights statement is recorded as written rather than generalized beyond this access route.

This specific Copyright-page restriction conflicts with the site's broad footer assertion that The Málaga Corpus is licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0; the restrictive statement governs current repository handling pending written clarification.
These terms are why no complete local manuscript-image cache was created.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviations in both witness streams
editorial_practices.representation: Expanded but marked in the near-diplomatic transcription
editorial_practices.description: The U-M editorial declaration says abbreviations are expanded and marked; the encoding also marks a small number of corrections, supplied readings, deletions, additions, and unclear readings.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Parallel versions in two manuscript witnesses
editorial_practices.representation: Sixty line-pair alignment units, Sloane above Hunter
editorial_practices.description: The transcription is laid out in pairs to facilitate comparison. This is an alignment of two raw transcriptions, not a reconstructed critical text or an assertion that the witnesses share one translation.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Longer Hunter On Bloodletting text at ff. 165v-166v
editorial_practices.representation: Only the opening hematoscopy passage shared with the Sloane transcription is included
editorial_practices.description: CME301 ends at Hunter f. 166r line 25 after the description of 'quesy and viscouse humours'; the scarifying and cupping continuation beginning at line 26 and all of f. 166v are excluded.

editorial_practices.representation: Expanded in a graphemic semi-diplomatic edition
editorial_practices.description: The article preserves folio layout, manuscript line division, original capitalization, punctuation, and word division while expanding abbreviations and minimizing editorial intervention.

editorial_practices.representation: Expanded letters are marked typographically in the static transcript pages
editorial_practices.description: The WebHunter target transcripts preserve foliation and line division while marking expanded letters in italic HTML.

Its manuscript figures are explicitly reproduced by permission of Glasgow University Library's Special Collections.

open_questions.question: Do the Sloane 3486, Hunter 307, and Royal 18 A VI versions descend from one Middle English translation or from distinct translations or recensions?
next_steps: Obtain or inspect Royal 18 A VI ff. 54r-55v and Sloane 3486 f. 147v images.
Collate all three complete components against the relevant Latin passage and consult later transmission scholarship.

open_questions.question: Are further Middle English witnesses of this exact On Bloodletting passage known beyond the three currently verified copies?
next_steps: Restore or obtain access to the eVK2 occurrence search for VK 0659.00 and audit related IMEP records.
Continue exact-incipit searches in current institutional catalogs and compare any candidate's opening and extent.
notes: The 2023 article reported that complete witness tracking remained uncertain; the current Royal catalog record supplies one additional witness with a closely corresponding incipit not discussed there.

open_questions.question: Where exactly within Royal MS 18 A VI ff. 54r-55v does the CME301-equivalent Three Inspections section end?
next_steps: Request a reference image of ff. 54r-55v from the British Library and align the text with CME301.
Record precise line or folio-side boundaries if the comparison confirms them.

open_questions.question: Does WebHunter's footer-level CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 statement apply to the exact Hunter 307 transcription and manuscript JPEGs despite the more specific Copyright page's permission requirements?
next_steps: Ask the WebHunter project or University of Málaga to identify in writing which components the footer license covers and whether the specific Copyright-page restrictions remain operative.
For manuscript images, also confirm reuse terms with Glasgow Archives & Special Collections and any identified rightsholders.
Until clarified, do not cache, redistribute, or publish the WebHunter transcription or JPEGs in reliance on the footer license.

open_questions.question: Do the three overlapping WebHunter part sequences collectively photograph every physical surface of Hunter 307, including covers, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves, blanks, and edges?
next_steps: Ask Glasgow Archives & Special Collections or the WebHunter project for a canonical physical-object inventory.
Do not label the portal a complete physical-object facsimile until that inventory is reconciled with the part-based page sequences.

open_questions.question: Will Glasgow University Library and the WebHunter rightsholders authorize a private complete image cache or public repository reuse of Hunter 307 images?
next_steps: Request written terms separately for private research storage and for public or commercial repository reuse.
If permission is granted, preserve the response and build one audited, gitignored full-source image bundle with the CME301 folio mapping.

open_questions.question: Can complete public facsimiles or pre-existing institutional reproductions of Sloane MS 3486 or Royal MS 18 A VI be identified?
next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services whether legacy digitization, microfilm, or photography exists for either shelfmark.
If no reusable complete surrogate exists, obtain quotes specifying the whole physical object separately from the target folios.
